nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q8.alt: Question 8. How do paragraphs 2 and 3 develop a central idea in the article? Choices: A: They state that William was not able to attend school. B: They show how William learned things on his own. C: they explain that others did not care about William’s questions. D: They give examples of the types of books William read.

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q9.alt: Question 9. What does the phrase “pore over” mean as it is used in paragraph 3? Choices: A: write about B: glance at C: examine D: copy

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q10.alt: Question 10. Paragraph 6 develops the author’s central claim by showing that William Choices: A: was distracted by so many objects in the junkyard B: was resourceful in finding what he needed C: was able to make use of his limited time in school D: was excited to look for new projects

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q11.alt: Question 11. Which sentence best describes the relationship between William and the people who doubted him? Choices: A: William was inspired and delighted by them. B: William ignored and then convinced them. C: William was concerned and worried about them. D: William listened to and then got help from them.

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q12.alt: Question 12. In paragraph 14, what does the word “marvel” suggest? Choices: A: They are amazed by what William has done. B: They are curious to see what will happen to the bulb. C: they are worried that William’s experiment may be dangerous. D: They are ready to have more lights in the house.

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q13.alt: Question 13. Which quotation shows an effect of success on William’s life? Choices: A: “Shoppers, farmers, and traders could see William’s tower from the local market.” (paragraph 10) B: “From the top of the tower he explained that, by using the power of wind, his machine could create electricity.” (paragraph 11) C: “William knew this was his moment—his moment to show everyone he wasn’t crazy . . .” (paragraph 12) D: “The story of the boy with only an eighth-grade education who’d built ‘electric wind’ spread across the Internet.” (paragraph 16)

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q14.alt: Question 14. Based on the information in the article, which of the following is most likely the author’s point of view? Choices: A: Access to formal schooling is important for success. B: Science education is needed to help people flourish. C: Curiosity and persistence can lead to change. D: Families are the best support system.

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q15.alt: Question 15. What role do paragraphs 1 through 3 mainly play in the story? Choices: A: They clarify the emotions that the two architects are feeling. B: They foreshadow the surprise at the ending. C: They set the scene and explain the process for picking a new design. D: They set a humorous tone by describing the two architects sitting in school desks.

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q16.alt: Question 16. In paragraph 6, “markedly” means doing something in Choices: A: an obvious way B: a careful way C: a respectful way D: an enthusiastic way

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q17.alt: Question 17. How does the word choice in paragraphs 9 and 11 affect the story? Choices: A: It describes how similar the design is to the Capitol building. B: It shows why the design is practical for a school. C: It conveys how impressive Mr. Kowalski’s design is. D: It explains what classical architecture looks like.

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q18.alt: Question 18. How do paragraphs 4 and 5 connect to what the reader learns in paragraph 18? Choices: A: They reveal that Mr. Kowalski thinks Holling’s father is a better architect than he is. B: They support the idea that Mr. Kowalski is guilty of stealing Mr. Hoodhood’s plans. C: They show that Mr. Kowalski hopes Holling likes his plan for the school. D: They provide information about how Mr. Kowalski’s and Mr. Hoodhood’s designs are different.

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q19.alt: Question 19. What do paragraphs 15 through 18 reveal about Holling? Choices: A: He disobeys his father easily. B: He did not know he was being deceived. C: He is unconcerned with the opinions of others. D: He is a dishonest person.

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q20.alt: Question 20. How does the author develop Holling’s point of view in the story? Choices: A: by explaining how his father sees the competition B: by describing Mr. Kowalski’s plans for the school building C: by describing his observations until he realizes what has happened D: by explaining the architectural review process

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q21.alt: Question 21. Which detail about Mr. Kowalski and Mr. Hoodhood would be most important to include in a summary of the story? Choices: A: They have model buildings under sheets on the table. B: They are competing to design a new school. C: They must meet with the school board at a school. D: They have to wait to hear who has been chosen.

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q29.alt: Question 29. How does paragraph 3 relate to the other paragraphs in the section “Unlikely in the Extreme”? Choices: A: Paragraph 3 introduces a problem, and the other paragraphs explain the solution to the problem. B: Paragraph 3 explains an idea, and the other paragraphs describe how the idea was disproved. C: Paragraph 3 presents an argument, and the other paragraphs give evidence to support the argument. D: Paragraph 3 sets up a comparison, and the other paragraphs give details about the comparison.

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q30.alt: Question 30. What role does paragraph 8 play in the organization of the article? Choices: A: It shows techniques that scientists use to study remote regions. B: It explains how the environment on Jupiter might support life. C: It describes the activities of scientists interested in extreme environments. D: It introduces the connection between activities on Earth and research in outer space.

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q31.alt: Question 31. How does paragraph 10 develop a central idea in the article? Choices: A: It explains how life in an extreme environment could be possible. B: It describes scientific research on the possibility of life in an extreme environment. C: It provides clues to the possible existence of life in an extreme environment. D: It presents questions to be answered about the possibility of life in an extreme environment.

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q32.alt: Question 32. How did scientists address the concern described in paragraph 14? Choices: A: They removed an ice core from the lake to study it. B: They applied chemicals to melt ice and keep the borehole open. C: they obtained a water sample with clean technology. D: They found sediment at the bottom of the lake.

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q33.alt: Question 33. What is the meaning of the word “contaminate” as used in paragraphs 14 and 16? Choices: A: color B: replace C: freeze D: pollute

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q34.alt: Question 34. According to the information in this article, which sentence describes how life was discovered beneath Antarctica? Choices: A: Scientists found proof of water beneath Antarctic ice, then they drilled to get samples of the water to look for life. B: Scientists saw signs of ice that may cover water on other planets, so they drilled for water beneath Antarctic ice. C: Scientists knew that there were life forms in the deep parts of the ocean, so they concluded that there was life in the water beneath Antarctic ice. D: Scientists found microbes on the surface of Antarctic ice, then they drilled into the ice to look for microbes beneath the surface.

nysed-ela-2019-g7-mc-q35.alt: Question 35. Which sentence best shows the author’s point of view on the topic of the article? Choices: A: The amount of water below the ice in Antarctica is surprising. B: Astronomers are excited about signs of ice and water on other planets. C: Antarctica is one of the most important scientific environments on Earth. D: The formation of very flat ice at the Vostok station is an important clue.
